name: Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop
name_original: Monte del Enebral — circular desde Santo Domingo de Silos
description: Cycle the moderate 25.0-mile (40.3 km) Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop, gaining 2201 feet (671 metres).
region_intro: Mountain biking around Santo Domingo De Silos offers diverse landscapes within the province of Burgos, Spain. The region is characterized by dramatic limestone mountains, river valleys like the Tabladillo, and significant natural features such as the Gorge of La Yecla. Extensive juniper forests are found within the Natural Area of the Sabinares de Arlanza, providing varied terrain for mountain bike trails. The area features a network of routes catering to different skill levels, from moderate rides to challenging trails. Best mountain bike trails around Santo Domingo De Silos The most popular mountain bike trail is Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop, a 25.0 miles (40.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 33 minutes to complete. This moderate route traverses varied terrain, including parts of the historic Camino del Cid. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Mamolar Village – Hermitage of Santa Cecilia loop from La Yecla, a difficult 44.0 miles (70.8 km) path. This challenging route features significant elevation gain and passes through scenic natural areas. Local mountain bikers also love the Hermitage of Santa Cecilia – Sad Hill Cemetery loop from Santo Domingo de Silos, a 37.0 miles (59.5 km) trail leading through the Sabinares de Arlanza Natural Park, often completed in about 5 hours 35 minutes. Mountain biking around Santo Domingo De Silos is defined by limestone formations, juniper forests, and river gorges. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Santo Domingo De Silos are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 5 reviews. Nearly 800 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Santo Domingo De Silos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available in Santo Domingo de Silos?
A: There are over 25 mountain bike trails around Santo Domingo de Silos, offering a variety of routes for different skill levels. The komoot community has explored nearly 800 mountain biking tours in the area.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in Santo Domingo de Silos?
A: The region generally offers good conditions for mountain biking for much of the year. Spring and autumn are often ideal, providing pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summers can be warm, while winters might bring cooler weather, but many trails remain accessible.

Q: Are there mountain bike trails for different skill levels?
A: Yes, Santo Domingo de Silos features a diverse network of trails catering to various abilities. You'll find routes ranging from moderate rides, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1100827857/camino-del-cid-and-enebral-de-carazo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop&lt;/a&gt;, to more challenging paths like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20150288" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mamolar Village – Hermitage of Santa Cecilia loop from La Yecla&lt;/a&gt;, which requires advanced fitness and riding skills.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes?
A: Many of the mountain bike routes in Santo Domingo de Silos are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1100827857/camino-del-cid-and-enebral-de-carazo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20150201" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Santa Cecilia – Sad Hill Cemetery loop from Santo Domingo de Silos&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural attractions can I see while mountain biking in Santo Domingo de Silos?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can explore the dramatic limestone formations of the Gorge of La Yecla, with its suspended walkways and rockpools, or ride through the extensive juniper forests of the Natural Area of the Sabinares de Arlanza. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4997641" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Peña de Carazo&lt;/a&gt; also offers stunning panoramic vistas.

Q: Are there historical or cultural sites to explore near the mountain bike trails?
A: Absolutely. The area is steeped in history. You can visit the renowned &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2521846" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monasterio de Santo Domingo de Silos&lt;/a&gt;, famous for its Romanesque cloister and Gregorian chants. Film enthusiasts might recognize the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2891679" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sad Hill Cemetery&lt;/a&gt;, a filming location for 'The Good, the Bad and the Ugly,' which is part of a popular loop trail.

Q: Is there parking available at the trailheads?
A: While specific parking details for every trailhead are not always listed, Santo Domingo de Silos and nearby villages typically offer public parking areas. It's advisable to check local signage or use navigation apps for the most convenient parking spots near your chosen starting point.

Q: Can I reach the mountain bike trails by public transport?
A: Santo Domingo de Silos is a rural area, and public transport options might be limited. While there may be bus services connecting to larger towns, direct public transport to specific trailheads is generally not common. Traveling by car is often the most flexible option for accessing the trails.

Q: Are the mountain bike trails in Santo Domingo de Silos suitable for families?
A: While many trails in the region are moderate to difficult, there are a few easier options available. For family outings, look for routes explicitly marked as 'easy' or those with minimal elevation gain and technical sections. Always check the route details and difficulty grade before heading out with children.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the mountain bike trails?
A: Many outdoor areas in Spain are dog-friendly, especially on trails. However, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially in natural parks like the Sabinares de Arlanza, to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other trail users. Be mindful of local regulations and ensure your dog is well-behaved and can handle the terrain.

Q: What do other mountain bikers say about the trails in Santo Domingo de Silos?
A: The mountain bike trails in Santo Domingo de Silos are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 5.0 stars from over 5 reviews. Riders often praise the varied terrain, stunning natural landscapes, and the blend of challenging sections with scenic views.

Q: Which mountain bike trails offer the best views?
A: For breathtaking views, consider routes that traverse the Natural Area of the Sabinares de Arlanza or ascend to viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3568119" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Peña Alta&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24551541" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Peña de Carazo loop from Santo Domingo de Silos&lt;/a&gt; is another excellent choice for panoramic vistas of the surrounding Mediterranean landscape.

Q: Are there any challenging mountain bike trails for experienced riders?
A: Yes, experienced riders will find plenty of challenging options. Trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20150288" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mamolar Village – Hermitage of Santa Cecilia loop from La Yecla&lt;/a&gt;, spanning over 70 km with significant elevation gain, offer demanding rides. The region's topography, with its limestone mountains and varied terrain, provides ample opportunities for advanced mountain bikers.
tips: The Monasterio de Santo Domingo de Silos is considered to be one of the most culturally and historically significant monasteries in Spain. If you are lucky, during a visit to the monastery you will be able to enjoy the world-famous Gregorian chants of the monks, who still cultivate and practice them today as ever. An album released by the monks was in the US charts for 53 weeks and went platinum three times.
Fun trail with somewhat demanding ups and downs.
Small nucleus of stone houses between hills and cultivated fields. The village is full of traditional houses with red roofs.
Small religious temple built at the end of the 9th or beginning of the 10th century. The hermitage has a single nave topped by a flat head made of ashlar stone and sillarejo.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this mountain biking loop is quite varied. You'll encounter a mix of untarmacked rural roads and tracks, which are generally in good condition, though some sections might be stony or muddy, especially after rain. There are also natural tracks and narrow trails, sometimes less than two meters wide with irregular surfaces. A small portion may include asphalted secondary roads with minimal traffic. About 88% of the route follows hiking trails, with some MTB alternatives utilizing country or minor roads.

faqs.question: Is the Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This loop is classified as moderate difficulty. While the mountain biking routes on the Camino del Cid are generally low to medium difficulty and accessible to cyclists familiar with long distances, some sections may demand more effort due to road type or inclines. You might occasionally need to dismount and walk short distances, but these challenging parts are usually brief. It's manageable for a wide range of riders, but not ideal for absolute beginners.

faqs.question: What historical sites or natural features will I encounter along the route?
faqs.answer: The loop offers a rich blend of natural beauty and historical significance. You'll pass by the impressive Monasterio de Santo Domingo de Silos, a significant religious building. Other highlights include the La Copeta Trail, the charming Mamolar Village, and the serene Hermitage of Santa Cecilia. The scenery includes valleys, moors, and mountains, with landscapes varying from green fields to more remote areas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to mountain bike this loop?
faqs.answer: The best seasons for mountain biking the Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop are typically spring and autumn. During these times, temperatures are milder and more pleasant for cycling. Summer can be very hot, and winter might bring colder temperatures and potentially more challenging conditions on unpaved sections.

faqs.question: Where does the Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: While the exact trailhead for this specific loop isn't explicitly defined, a common starting point for routes in this area, given the highlights, would be near Santo Domingo de Silos. As a well-known village with a monastery, you can generally find parking options within or on the outskirts of the village. It's advisable to check local signage for designated parking areas.

faqs.question: Is the Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are allowed on trails within the Camino del Cid region, especially on rural tracks and paths. However, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, particularly when passing through villages, near livestock, or in areas with wildlife. Be sure to carry water for your dog, as water sources might be limited, and always clean up after your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this mountain biking loop?
faqs.answer: No, you do not typically need specific permits or pay entrance fees to mountain bike the Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop. The route traverses public paths and rural roads. However, as parts of the trail are within the Sabinares del Arlanza-La Yecla Natural Park, always respect park regulations, stay on marked paths, and practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: Does this loop connect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this loop intersects with several other official trails. Notably, it covers sections of the GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT, Camino de Santiago de la Lana.BTT, Camino de Santiago de la Lana.Senderista, El Destierro, Sendero Fuentes del Río Lobos, and Camino del Cid (Senderista). This offers opportunities to extend your ride or explore different routes if you wish.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a mountain bike ride on this loop?
faqs.answer: For this moderate mountain biking loop, you should bring essential gear including a well-maintained mountain bike, a helmet, sufficient water (especially in warmer months), snacks, a repair kit for punctures, and a first-aid kit. Given the varied terrain, appropriate cycling attire, including padded shorts, is recommended. A GPS device or a downloaded map of the route is also highly advisable.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or considerations for mountain biking within the Sabinares del Arlanza-La Yecla Natural Park?
faqs.answer: When mountain biking within the Sabinares del Arlanza-La Yecla Natural Park, it's important to adhere to general natural park regulations. This typically includes staying on marked trails to protect flora and fauna, respecting private property, and avoiding disturbing wildlife. Camping is usually restricted to designated areas, and open fires are prohibited. Always carry out all your trash to preserve the natural beauty of the park.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or resupply along the Camino del Cid and Enebral de Carazo Loop?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through or near small villages like Mamolar Village and starts near Santo Domingo de Silos. These settlements often have local cafes, restaurants, or small shops where you can find refreshments, food, and potentially resupply. However, it's always wise to carry enough provisions for the duration of your ride, especially between villages, as services might be infrequent.

waypoints.name: Monasterio de Santo Domingo de Silos
